expressions.name: batchnorm2d_training_forward
expressions.formula: The reference module is nn.BatchNorm2d(num_features=64) and is never switched to eval(), so PyTorch uses training-mode batch statistics for the forward output. Let X have shape (N,C,H,W)=(64,64,512,512), gamma=weight with shape (64), beta=bias with shape (64), and eps=1e-5. For each channel c, M=N*H*W=64*512*512. mean[c] = (1/M) * sum_{rn=0}^{63} sum_{rh=0}^{511} sum_{rw=0}^{511} X[rn,c,rh,rw]. var[c] = (1/M) * sum_{rn=0}^{63} sum_{rh=0}^{511} sum_{rw=0}^{511} (X[rn,c,rh,rw] - mean[c])^2. PyTorch BatchNorm2d training forward normalizes with the biased variance above. For every output element: Y[n,c,h,w] = gamma[c] * (X[n,c,h,w] - mean[c]) / sqrt(var[c] + eps) + beta[c]. With default initialization gamma[c]=1 and beta[c]=0, but implementations should still bind the affine parameters correctly.
expressions.boundary_treatment: No spatial padding or boundary extension is used. The reduction domain for each channel is exactly all elements over N,H,W. eps is added inside sqrt to avoid division by zero. running_mean and running_var are buffers updated by PyTorch during training, but they are not the statistics used to compute this forward output. Do not call eval() and do not implement inference-mode BatchNorm for this task.

running_mean.note: Updated by PyTorch training BatchNorm but not used to compute the forward output.

running_var.note: Updated by PyTorch training BatchNorm but not used to compute the forward output.

expressions.lowering_contract: Implement training-mode BatchNorm2d. A correct Triton lowering needs at least a per-channel reduction over N*H*W for mean and variance, then an elementwise affine normalization pass. Do not use running_mean/running_var for output. Do not set the module to eval().
expressions.TDD_requirement: Compare against torch.nn.BatchNorm2d(num_features=64) in its default training mode on input shape (64,64,512,512). Check that output shape equals input shape and that each channel has been normalized using current-batch statistics over N,H,W. A small-shape smoke test should include N>1 and H*W>1 to catch accidental inference-mode implementations.
